content.problemStatement:
Chef always likes to play with arrays. He came up with a new term **"magical subarray"**. A subarray is called magical if its starting index is **1** (1-based indexing). Now, Chef has an array of **N** elements and 2 types of queries:



**type 1:** Given two numbers **i** and **x**, the value at index **i** should be updated to **x**.
**type 2:** Given two numbers **i** and **k**, your program should output the total number of magical subarrays with the last index ≤ **i** in which the xor of all elements is equal to **k**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers N and Q denoting the number of elements in the array and the number of queries respectively. The second line contains N space-separated integers A1, A2 ... AN denoting the initial values of the array. Each of the following Q lines describes an operation. If the first integer is 1, it means that the operation is of type 1 and it will be followed by two integers i and x. If the first integer is 2, it means that the operations is of type 2 and it will be followed by two integers i and k.
content.outputFormat: For each operation of type 2, print the number of magical subarrays on a separate line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first query of type 2 checks for the number of magical subarrays with XOR equal to 1. There are three such subarrays: [1], [1, 1, 1, 1] and [1, 1, 1, 1, 1]. The second query of type 1 updates the value at index 3 to 2. The third query of type 2 checks for the number of magical subarrays with XOR equal to 1. There is one such subarray: [1].

The first query of type 2 checks for the number of magical subarrays with XOR equal to 2. There are two such subarrays: [2] and [1, 2, 3]. The second query of type 1 updates the value at index 2 to 4. The third query of type 2 checks for the number of magical subarrays with XOR equal to 2. There are no such subarrays.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a prefix sum array to store the XOR of all elements up to each index. Then, for each query of type 2, we can iterate over all possible subarrays ending at index i and check if the XOR of the subarray is equal to k. We can use the prefix sum array to calculate the XOR of each subarray in O(1) time.
solution.timeComplexity: O(Q * N) — because for each query, we are iterating over all possible subarrays ending at index i.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — because we are storing the prefix sum array of size N.
